items.q: Is an algorithm the same thing as a computer program?
items.correct: No - an algorithm is the abstract sequence of steps; a program is one implementation of it in a particular language. The same algorithm can be coded many ways.
items.misconception: Yes - algorithm is just the formal word for program code.
items.random_wrong: Yes - algorithms only exist once they are typed into a computer.
items.why: Students meet algorithms through code, so the idea fuses with its implementation. A recipe followed by hand is still an algorithm - no computer required.

items.q: Does an IF statement repeat its block of code?
items.correct: No - IF is selection: it decides once whether to execute the block; repetition needs an iteration construct such as WHILE or FOR.
items.misconception: Yes - IF keeps running its block until the condition turns false.
items.random_wrong: Yes - IF and WHILE are interchangeable keywords.
items.why: Both test conditions, inviting the mix-up. WHILE loops back to retest after each pass; IF tests once and moves on - tracing both makes the difference vivid.

items.q: Do flowchart symbols mean whatever the author wants?
items.correct: No - the symbols are standardised: ovals for start/end, rectangles for processes, diamonds for decisions, parallelograms for input/output.
items.misconception: Yes - any shape can represent any kind of step.
items.random_wrong: Yes - only the arrows matter, the shapes are decoration.
items.why: Shapes feel cosmetic until a mark scheme penalises them. The convention lets any reader parse the logic instantly, which is the whole point of flowcharts.

items.correct: A precise sequence of steps that solves a problem
items.why: It's A precise sequence of steps that solves a problem. “Any piece of computer hardware” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: An if-else structure is an example of:
items.why: Correct answer: Selection. “Sequence” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Repeating a block of steps while a condition is true is called:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Iteration. “Selection” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In a flowchart, input and output are shown using which symbol?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A parallelogram. “A diamond” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An algorithm doubles its input and then adds 3. What is the output when the input is 5?
items.why: Exact answer: 13. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
